extra.message: User-controlled input from request.POST.get('first_name', '') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(). An attacker can craft a malicious base64 payload to achieve arbitrary remote code execution on the server.

Fallback Python 2 code execution path: user-controlled input is base64-decoded and passed to exec(). This is the same vulnerability as line 72 but with a different decoding strategy, allowing arbitrary code execution.

User-controlled filename from request.GET.get('p') is joined with base_path and opened without validation. Although os.path.join rejects absolute paths starting with '/', it does NOT prevent directory traversal using '../' sequences, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the filesystem (e.g. ../../settings.py).

User-controlled URL from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to Django's redirect() function with no validation. An attacker can craft a link that redirects users to arbitrary external malicious sites, enabling phishing attacks.

User-controlled 'fwd' parameter from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up a function by name from globals() and then invoked with function(request). This allows an attacker to call any view function in the module, such as 'admin' or 'boom', bypassing intended access controls.

The qs query parameter is rendered into a JavaScript context using the |safe filter, which disables Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by breaking out of the string assignment, leading to reflected XSS.

The URL path parameter is rendered into a JavaScript context using the |safe filter, which disables Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious path, leading to reflected XSS.

The qs query parameter is rendered into an HTML input value attribute using the |safe filter, which disables Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can break out of the attribute by injecting a quote character and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript, leading to reflected XSS.

The qs query parameter is rendered into a JavaScript context using the |safe filter, which disables Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious qs value, leading to reflected XSS.

The csrf_image view is decorated with @csrf_exempt, disabling Django's CSRF protection. Combined with a reflected XSS vulnerability in the same view (csrf/image.html line 48 using |safe), this enables a CSRF-based attack chain: an attacker can XSS-inject an image tag that triggers a GET/POST request to execute actions on behalf of the victim.

Django's CSRF middleware (CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES, meaning no view in the application has CSRF protection by default. Combined with the globally disabled X-Frame-Options middleware, this makes all form-based actions on the site vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.

The dor_user_profile view takes a userid from the URL path and retrieves user data directly without any authentication or authorization check. Any user can access and modify any other user's profile (including admin users) by simply changing the numeric userid in the URL (e.g., /direct-object-references/users/2).

The dor_user_profile view accepts POST requests to update user profile data (name, email) with no authentication or session check. There is no login mechanism anywhere in the application, and the view lacks any user identification or ownership verification.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The missing_access_control view gates admin functionality based solely on a GET parameter (?action=admin). No authentication, session, or role verification is performed. An attacker can trivially access the admin page by changing the action parameter.

The boom view unconditionally raises an Exception. With DEBUG=True in settings.py, Django will display a detailed debug page including the full stack trace, local variables, settings, and environment information, leading to significant information disclosure.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message

extra.message: DEBUG is set to True and TEMPLATE_DEBUG is also enabled. In production, this exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, Django settings, and environment variables to end users. This is a critical security misconfiguration.

A hardcoded, weak Django SECRET_KEY is committed to the repository. This key is used for session signing, CSRF tokens, and other cryptographic operations. An attacker with access to this key can forge session cookies, CSRF tokens, and other signed data.

Django's X-Frame-Options middleware (XFrameOptionsMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. Without this middleware, the application does not set the X-Frame-Options header, making it vulnerable to clickjacking attacks where an attacker can embed the site in an iframe and trick users into performing actions.

cwe: CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

extra.message: The 'monster' cookie is set with no Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite flags. Without the HttpOnly flag, the cookie is accessible via JavaScript, making it vulnerable to theft via XSS. Without the Secure flag, the cookie is sent over unencrypted HTTP connections.

The debug toolbar can be enabled by any user by simply adding a 'debug' query parameter, POST parameter, or cookie. This exposes detailed debugging information including SQL queries, HTTP headers, settings, and request data to any user of the application.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
